bibliographic_data.title: Reverse Online Guessing Attacks on PAKE Protocols
bibliographic_data.abstract: Though not yet widely deployed, password-authenticated key exchange (PAKE) protocols have been the subject of several recent standardization efforts, partly because of their resistance against various guessing attacks but also because they do not require a public-key infrastructure (PKI), making them naturally resistant against PKI failures. The goal of this paper is to reevaluate the PAKE model by noting that the absence of a PKI—or, more generally, of a mechanism aside from the password for authenticating the server—makes such protocols vulnerable to reverse online guessing attacks, in which an adversary attempts to validate password guesses by impersonating a server. While their logic is similar to traditional guessing, where the attacker impersonates a client, reverse guessing poses a unique risk because the burden of detection is shifted to the clients, rendering existing defenses against traditional guessing moot.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper introduces reverse online guessing attacks on PAKE protocols and demonstrates that server authentication is crucial to prevent such attacks, which are not effectively mitigated by traditional client-side defense mechanisms.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors analyze the security of PAKE protocols in the absence of PKI and present a comprehensive description of reverse online guessing attacks. They use the EKE protocol as an example while highlighting general structures applicable to other theoretical and practical PAKE protocols.
outcomes: Reverse guessing is particularly effective when attacking clients indiscriminately, such as in phishing or password-spraying attacks.
Stakeholders should authenticate the server using more stringent measures than just the user’s password.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper discusses the security of PAKE protocols and introduces reverse online guessing attacks. It highlights that traditional client-side defense mechanisms are ineffective against these attacks due to the shift in detection burden from servers to clients. The authors provide a detailed analysis and suggest that server authentication should be more stringent than just using the user’s password.
